item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this one. Some people might not like how the clinical tone of the Foundation was swapped out for a more objective and personal one, but it kind of reminds me of ●●|●●●●●|●●|● or 2439, where the format of the SCP article has to have a different tone because of the nature of the discovery, and it's weird to think of all the crazy bullcrap this SCP has to pull in order to make it this irredeemable. +1 from me!

item.title: My View
item.content:encoded:
I'm confused.
I read it and don't know what it is, what it should be or what the idea is.
I read the comments and it get the idea what the background is.

I like the concept, but to read the comments on a SCP, just to get the point of a SCP… not so good.
I look if there are tales, so I can change my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been thinking about this one all day, but not for good reasons.

Downvote. There is a solid idea there, but nothing done with it, and I don't like that.

The Containment procedures look like they are written for an entirely different object, one that needs to be ignored to be contained, an idea that I personally like.

The description… ugh, for instance it looks like 3 different entities wrote it. I don't mind if it is not entirely cold and calculated like a foundation research document should be, but it just feels like every other line is written by a different entity (you know that's not a bad idea…) and since when does the foundation grant absolution?

That's the other part that bothers me about this. This is coming off like it's from that universe where there was that version of the foundation that is a church.

This whole article comes across as if it was written by 3 people who were snubbing a petulant child to punish it. "It's been a naughty scp so it's not getting any attention!" And it annoys me because there are several good concepts here.

Also, how does ignoring it contain it? It's some anomaly that is free to do whatever it wants because the foundation, and whatever other powerful agencies are not giving it any attention, which seems very counter to the foundation's goals. Maybe that concept could work if it was to contain a different anomaly. SCP-A must be ignored to contain SCP-B kinda thing.

Ugh, I just really don't like this one, because I COULD like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know how to say my feeling when I saw this article.

so I upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like ones that play with your head a bit, leave you guessing and make you draw some of your own conclusions. The problem with this one is that it…doesn't have anything to play with? I enjoy 579 because there's toys that let you frame what you think it might be…here, it's just "it's something and we can't/won't tell just because." It feels more like an SCP prompt than an SCP itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They know what they did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The problem is, there's always going to be someone out there who thinks it isn't that bad. There's no way to make something universally horrible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's short, sure. From what I know it was apart of a short story contest, so it does that job perfectly well.
But I don't think it has enough there to warrant it being so short. The addendum really does the article in for me. "There will be no forgiveness." It's too informal. I can't believe that that would be written in an official document.
Of course, as others have pointed out, that might be the entire point. That what whatever this thing was did something so abhorrent that it being so informal was deserved. All that does for me, though, it just make me wonder "Why?" but not in a good way. It's more an unsatisfied "Why?" then a "Holy shit…why?". I'd like to see something to do with this skip that explains what it did, and the author could have a ball making it as horrid and terrifying as possible. Then (if it's written well enough), going back to read this entry, I'll think that the informal tone was deserved.
Until then, I have to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm reminded of Elisha ben Abuya, aka "the other one".1 He saw some people climbing trees to steal eggs kill/not kill the birds and fall/not fall out of the tree, and when those things happened in the wrong combination2 he declared war on God and started breaking every biblical tenet he could find. Apparently God singled him out specifically as being exempt from redemption.3

Footnotes
1. Or more specifically his depiction in Unsong, since I'm not familiar enough with the Talmud to judge how true to the source it is.
2. Kill bird+not fall out of tree; not kill bird+fall out of tree. This was unfair because the guy who killed the bird wasn't punished but the guy who "only" stole its eggs was, and therefore the other one concluded that God is completely wrong about morality.
3. The Talmud, I'm told, is weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, short, sweet and interestesting. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I fall into the "Don't get it" category. Downvoted, and I'll try to explain why.
-055 is an unknown, approached logically by the Foundation in its attempts to know the unknowable.
The correlation with -2998 isn't exact, and -2998 is the SIGNAL, not the alien itself. But I see the link people are making there. Still, It's at least knowable, and the Foundation has contained the signal, as much as can reasonably be assured.
-902 is an obvious cognitohazard. It stays as unknown as it is because for it to be otherwise to to enhance its danger level, to relax the containment (which, for pretty much every cognitohazard, is keeping it unknown - although for most of them you can know in general the hazard and the containment… 902 is 'special' that way)
-579 …is not something to emulate, imo. It is "Big scary thing we can't know about and might have to try and destroy again"… and this doesn't even reach THAT level.

Basically this is, "It's been a very naughty Skip, and that's all anyone shall ever know." </montypython>
…which works for humor. This could easily have been written with a slight tonal shift for a -J, and I'd probably approve. But the very fact that the Foundation doesn't (apparently) contain this… doesn't WANT to, and doesn't want ANYONE to know a damn thing about it… goes against everything The Foundation stands for.

Sorry, but I can't accept that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's kinda like 055 in regards to not knowing whatever the hell it is but something implied. The addendum is also interesting but there is nothing for me to go off of. I'm I missing something? What I am getting is that it's an unknown anomaly with a file that references nothing in regards to said anomaly besides speculate. Then the implication that this did something wrong and left to rot.

Honestly, I feel like it needs expanding upon. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Harsh. Here, have a +1.

Have to say that I'm surprised that so many folks have questioned the nature of 2156's transgression and the likelihood of universal consensus and the non-clinical tone and all that when the answer seems pretty obvious to me:

The Foundation probably lied. The transgression is likely irrelevant and may not even have happened. The universe as a whole almost certainly didn't have a unanimous vote on the issue, but that also probably doesn't really matter. Despite the non-clinical tone, this seems very in keeping with the Foundation's MO to me because most likely they just wrote up exactly what was required to put 2165 in containment, regardless if it was true or not. They do this all the time, after all, it's just that this time, the cover story is built into the SCP article itself instead of being told to an amnestitized public.

At least, that's my own personal head-cannon (kaboom kaboom), so take it for what it's worth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Makes things too boring, I suppose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why can't other SCPs be contained this way?

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
I love the sense of foreboding of this skip. What did it do? How was it undone? Mmm, love it.

https://youtu.be/zVakeoUPOhM

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. It strikes a note within me, but I can't say that I like the article, or think that it's particularly well written. I think I can't bring myself to vote on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The more time that passes after the short story contest, the more I like this one.

I think I finally figured out why: not only does it have that cold anger to it, it reads like the Foundation's replacement for a proclamation formerly carved on a pillar in pictograms. Maybe it's a punishment that feels fair.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: found some pixiv fan art of this

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: As a fan of this, I definitely understand what you mean, it's ok.

I don't get it.

I mean, I get it. I get the point of the article. I know why it exists. I know why people like it. But I don't GET IT. And I don't like it, either. Maybe it's just a bad day, but this just seems lazy to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author here: the word "reference" refers to the article, or SCP document itself- i.e the only mention of SCP-2165 that can exist in the universe, since it's being held in a space outside of both regular space and time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Translator from the Korean Wiki. What does the word "reference" refer to in this article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Headcanon time:
It's possible that the Foundation universe is one in which various alien intelligences exist, each of them with their own morality. Thus, every possible action can be forgiven by one of these intelligences. The exception to this is SCP-2165. So what is SCP-2165? I believe that SCP-2165 is itself, or at least perception of itself(be it physical or mental). Attempting to think of an unforgivable act is itself an unforgivable act. That's why we can't know what it is, because telling people about the unforgivable act is unforgivable.
And then philosophy happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This misses one of the biggest draws for me about these write-ups, which is the contextualization of huge ideas. Without a human response, this is just a bad thing that happened. It's like Sherlock with no Watson, we don't really get much flavor or a human side to it. I appreciate this as a minimum word count entry, but I don't think it stands alone.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I think Snoozee is a good SCP-2165 contender, I stand in my position to think that SCP-2165 is the entity once know as "The ██████████", who almost erased SCP Foundation and probably reality itself before was left Irredeemable.

The entity could have been an Apollyon Class Object, and the Foundation somehow averted a XK-Class scenario successfully. The problem is, now that anything about SCP-2165 is Irredeemable, the method used to counter it is now forever lost to Foundation, so for further menaces like SCP-2317-K they have yet to figure out another option.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here are my philosophical/logical issues with this:

1) The implication is that there is an action so abhorrent that reality itself rebels against that action. If that is the case, then could anything perform that action? I mean, if revulsion against that act was built into the fabric of existence, then how could anything that exists perform it?

2) One of the purposes of punishment is to serve as an example to others so that they don't commit the infraction as well. If this action is something that a being can choose to perform, then not telling anyone what the infraction was is counterproductive. If your response to that is, "It is so bad than any being considering it would instinctively know how bad it is", then I would direct you to my first point. If this action is so bad that it would be repulsive to even the worst things contained by the Foundation, then how could any being choose to do it at all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had an interesting thought regarding this. It says that it was judged irredeemable by "universal consensus". There are, IMO, two ways to interpret this:

  • Literally everything in the universe, sapient or not, physical or not, agreed that it is/was irredeemable.
  • The universe itself decided that it is/was irredeemable.

In either case, the prosecutors have decided that nothing is to interact with it in any way. It can interact with literally nothing in any way, shape, or form. Not even light or gravity interact with it.1

Its punishment is total and eternal banishment from all things. It's left to its own devices because there's literally nothing it can do. This thing, whatever it is/was, is now Absent. That's just as terrifying as whatever it did to earn that punishment.

Footnotes
1. In the Foundation!verse, we know that light/color/electromagnetism can be sapient, so why not the other fundamental forces?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is honestly the most scary SCP. The idea of a action so foul that nobody ever knows about it until some one does it is horrifying. 1+

EDIT : fixed misspellings

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I really don't see that, my initial impression was of something very ancient. Although your version does make a bit more sense. Even if that is the case, what the purpose of this document? Who wrote it? Who is reading it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hence:

at one point

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Except that it isn't contained ("left to its own devices") and this document isn't in the Foundation's databases but rather is in some completely isolated pocket universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…My headcanon is still that it's Snoozee.

(Note for future readers: if you don't know who Snoozee is, that's right, you don't.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It does kind of have a feel like whatever decided that this thing was utterly irredeemable has some kind of awareness of SCP documentation as part of the "right" way to handle crimes against nature. Less "we are containing this" and more "forces beyond Foundation control have decided that Containment Is Appropriate, possibly without really comprehending what that means."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, at one point, it must have been an SCP object proper, before whatever happened to it made it Irredeemable. The forces governing the universe probably altered the document and containment procedures, which we then had to follow (or maybe it was even by force; who knows?).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think that it suffers from comparison to SCP-055, but I do still fail to understand why it is an SCP document. This isn't contained by the Foundation is it? This is a being escoriated and anathema to some vast cosmic force, which uses the Foundation's format to describe it, but terribly off-tone, for some reason. I just don't get what this document is even supposed to be. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably the reason why this got compared to 055 is that, in addition to some thematic similarities, this was written for a contest where the goal was to write entries in 500 words or under (bonus points for writing it under 237 words). This had many people use the route of "we can't write about it so that's why the entry is so short", which lead to a lot of memetic skips with similarities to 055.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it.

I'll admit I'm a little bewildered by the fact that people keep comparing it negatively to 055; the end result is similar but they arrive there by completely different routes, and honestly I love the contrast? We'd love to know what 055 actually is, because it's potentially either very useful or a ticking time bomb or both, but we can't remember that information long enough to interpret it. The best we can do is hold onto a few minor details of what it isn't and hope that it's not planning anything from the safety of its anonymity.

Whereas the universe at large, and by extension the Foundation, doesn't want to know anything about 2165. The less we have to think about it the happier we'll all be. Because it's awful. Just the worst. Whatever it was going to do it's already done and the universe had to clean up after it and now it has to go sit in the cosmic time-out corner for all eternity because we just can't with it right now.

And for some reason I think that's hilarious. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I forgive.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to simply call this article pretentious and move on, but that wouldn't be helpful to anyone.

We've had articles in the past which create their intended effect by withholding information. These articles have to convince us of something without directly stating it. If too much is withheld, the article is reduced to trying to convince us of something without any actual proof. It seems like it's to appear more important, compelling, or interesting than it actually is.

You're telling us that this thing is irredeemable for some reason, but instead of hinting at why or how, you give us absolutely nothing.

I hate use SCP-579 in a debate, but look at that article. The only thing it does to convince us that it's dangerous is by repeating [DATA EXPUNGED] ad nauseum. This article doesn't do anything to convince us of its own point. I may be one of 579's detractors, but even I have to admit that it tries to give us something to try and convince us. This article does nothing.

Tomode said it best: "There's leaving things to the imagination, and then there's wrapping an empty box in toilet paper and claiming it's full of gold." This article is what you get when you don't even bother wrapping it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, I think the article is exactly as long as it needs to be. Objections have been raised in other posts, but I like the ambiguity displayed here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted.

The whole point of [DATA EXPUNGED] is that it's effective in selective quantities. The more you abuse it, the less effective it becomes. This is an SCP that is entirely expunged data.

And yes, SCP-055 exists, but that's the problem, there already is an SCP like this. Moreover, unlike SCP-055, the reason why everything is unknown about it is not because the Foundation cannot figure anything out about it, but because the Foundation chooses not to say anything about it because it's angry about something the SCP did, which is completely out of character for the Foundation.

It also goes contradicts the internal logic of the piece. If the Foundation didn't want there to be anything written about it at all because they hate this thing that much, then why make a page on it? If you don't want it ever referenced again, then why reference it in the first place?

Not to mention if the Foundation can remove things from causality in a perfect prison where they can never escape, then why haven't they done that with other objects already? Especially 231, the whole premise of which is its containment procedure is the most morally abhorrent act possible?

Now, on the other hand, maybe this SCP is a cognitohazard that makes the Foundation really pissed off enough to go completely against character in the way that's being described. If so, it runs into the problem again of another SCP that already does that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A short format is not an excuse for negligible content. The repeated "it is whatever you want it to be" style articles are making me weary of authors taking credit for the reader's imagination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So basically whatever this thing is (or was) did something so heinous that the Universe itself chose to stop acknowledging it, except in the frame of contempt. So bad, in fact, that when the Foundation found out about it they reacted the same way. Being how clinical the Foundation normally is about other horrendous atrocities, this terrifies me. Even worse, what ever it is (or was), it has (or had) remorse for its actions that will never be accepted. There is so much unknown about this I can't help but be terrified at what it could have been. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay. I wish to say that this is, to me, on par with SCP-231, except with no blackboxes or redaction, and in about 75% less words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, even further than that! <G>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was one of the goals in writing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The alien being tortured and having to have its torture be broadcast for extremely vague crimes is probably the perceived similarly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-2000 is by FortuneFavorsBold. Eskobar's 2000 contest entry didn't win and therefore didn't get the 2000 slot, instead getting the SCP-2998 slot. I still don't see how they're similar though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is quite interesting, mainly because it allows you to fill almost all the blanks by yourself if you manage to keep the suspension of disbelief.

Personally I think that it lacked a certain something (easter egg, addendum or whatever) to give a better idea of what the hell is this. On the other hand I get that keeping it as vague as possible was kind of the point.

All in all, upvoted.

PS: I like how easy is to headcannon this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whatever you feel more comfortable with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funny, this. I think the last few words wrap the skip up in the most perfect way…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It reminds me of the punishment of 'shunning' in some early civilizations - but extrapolated to the universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does this object do it as well or better than 055 did it?

Yes.
(though I do like 055, mind)

I suggest creating a special thread so everyone can give ideas of what SCP-2165 possibly did.
rrh rrh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The addendum ruins this for me. I love the rest, but I can't upvote in confidence with those narmy as hell last 5 words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, Scantron, THANK YOU for saying it! I didn't dare! ;>
(though I do confess that my very first, spontaneous idea was one Adolf…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me, it doesn't matter who or what it is. I can imagine whatever I please - something different every day. And that's what I like about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But isn't this the best way to write a story for a short contest, Djoric? Make the shortness part of the plot if possible?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ???? Isn't SCP-2000 a building? What's it to do with this? 8-|
